items.subtopic: Chest Discomfort | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY
items.title: High Prevalence and Hospitalization Rate of Chest Discomfort in EDs
items.content: Chest discomfort is a leading cause for emergency department visits, with 6–7 million cases annually in the U.S. Over 60% are hospitalized for further testing.
keyPoints: Chest discomfort is one of the three most common reasons for ED visits in the United States.
Results in 6–7 million emergency visits each year.
More than 60% of patients with chest discomfort are hospitalized for additional testing.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · Chest Discomfort | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: Chest Discomfort | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY
items.title: Low Incidence of ACS Despite High Evaluation Rates
items.content: Despite extensive evaluation, fewer than 15% of patients with chest discomfort are diagnosed with acute coronary syndrome (ACS), highlighting the need for careful risk stratification.
keyPoints: Fewer than 15% of evaluated patients are eventually diagnosed with ACS.
Rates of ACS diagnosis range from 10–20% in unselected populations, and as low as 5% in some studies.
2–6% of patients discharged with presumed nonischemic chest discomfort later have a missed myocardial infarction.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · Chest Discomfort | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.title: Understanding Myocardial Ischemia: Mechanisms and Clinical Implications
items.content: Myocardial ischemia occurs when there is an imbalance between myocardial oxygen supply and demand, leading to chest discomfort known as angina pectoris. This condition can progress to myocardial infarction (MI) if the ischemia is severe and prolonged.
keyPoints: Imbalance between myocardial oxygen requirements and supply causes ischemia.
Ischemia may lead to MI if it persists for at least 20 minutes.
Angina pectoris is characterized by aching, heavy, squeezing, or crushing chest discomfort.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.title: Distinguishing Stable and Unstable Angina: Clinical Features and Diagnosis
items.content: Stable angina is predictable and relieved by rest or nitroglycerin, while unstable angina occurs with less exertion or at rest. Unstable ischemic heart disease includes unstable angina, NSTEMI, and STEMI, classified based on ECG findings.
keyPoints: Stable angina: discomfort begins gradually, reaches peak in minutes, relieved by rest/nitroglycerin.
Unstable angina: occurs with lower exertion or at rest, not relieved by rest.
ECG helps classify unstable ischemic heart disease into unstable angina, NSTEMI, and STEMI.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.title: Characteristics of Ischemic Chest Discomfort: Clinical Presentation
items.content: Ischemic chest discomfort, or angina, typically presents as retrosternal pain that may radiate to the left arm, neck, jaw, or shoulders. It is often described as aching or squeezing and can be severe in MI.
keyPoints: Chest discomfort is usually retrosternal with possible radiation to arms, neck, jaw, or shoulders.
Discomfort quality: aching, heavy, squeezing, or constricting; may vary among patients.
MI-related discomfort is more severe, lasts ≥30 minutes, and is not relieved by rest.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.title: Pain Mechanism in Pericarditis
items.content: The pain of pericarditis is primarily due to pleural inflammation rather than the pericardium itself, as most of the pericardial surfaces are insensitive. This results in pleuritic chest discomfort exacerbated by breathing or changes in position.
keyPoints: Pain arises from associated pleural inflammation.
Discomfort is usually pleuritic and worsened by breathing, coughing, or positional changes.
Referred pain to shoulder and neck due to overlapping sensory supply of the central diaphragm via the phrenic nerve.

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Acute Aortic Syndromes
items.content: Acute aortic syndromes present with severe, sudden-onset chest discomfort often described as 'tearing.' The location varies: midline anterior chest for ascending aorta involvement and back pain for descending aorta.
keyPoints: Presents with severe, sudden-onset chest discomfort, sometimes 'tearing'.
Ascending aortic syndromes cause midline anterior chest pain; descending involve back pain.
Commonly associated with hypertension or connective tissue diseases like Marfan syndrome.

items.title: Pulmonary Embolism Pain Characteristics
items.content: Pulmonary embolism can cause pleuritic chest discomfort due to pulmonary infarction, distended pulmonary artery, or right ventricular stress. Massive emboli may mimic myocardial infarction with severe substernal pain.
keyPoints: Chest discomfort is typically sudden and pleuritic.
Small emboli cause lateral, pleuritic pain; massive emboli can mimic MI with severe substernal pain.
Massive or submassive embolism may present with syncope, hypotension, and right heart failure signs.

items.subtopic: OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort
items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Pulmonary Causes of Chest Pain
items.content: Chest pain in pulmonary diseases often involves the pleura or surrounding structures. Pleurisy is characterized by a knifelike pain exacerbated by inspiration or coughing, whereas chronic pulmonary hypertension can mimic angina due to right ventricular myocardial ischemia.
keyPoints: Pleurisy: Knifelike pain worsened by inspiration or coughing.
Chronic pulmonary hypertension: Chest pain similar to angina, suggesting right ventricular myocardial ischemia.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort

items.subtopic: OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort
items.title: Assessment Priorities in Acute Nontraumatic Chest Discomfort
items.content: In patients presenting with acute nontraumatic chest discomfort, the initial clinical encounter should prioritize assessing patient stability and identifying life-threatening causes. High-risk conditions include ACS, acute aortic syndrome, pulmonary embolism, tension pneumothorax, pericarditis with tamponade, and fulminant myocarditis.
keyPoints: Assess patient's clinical stability and probability of life-threatening cause.
High-risk conditions: ACS, acute aortic syndrome, pulmonary embolism, tension pneumothorax, pericarditis with tamponade, fulminant myocarditis.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort

items.title: Quality of Pain in Chest Discomfort
items.content: The quality of chest discomfort is crucial for forming an initial clinical impression and assessing the likelihood of a serious cardiopulmonary process. Pressure or tightness often indicates myocardial ischemic pain, while pleuritic pain suggests pleural involvement.
keyPoints: Pressure or tightness may indicate myocardial ischemia.
Dyspnea or anxiety can accompany ischemic symptoms without 'pain'.
Pleuritic discomfort suggests pericarditis, pulmonary embolism, or parenchymal processes.
'Tearing' pain often indicates acute aortic dissection.
Burning quality may suggest acid reflux or myocardial ischemia.

items.title: Location of Discomfort in Chest Pain
items.content: The location and radiation pattern of chest discomfort are key to differentiating between cardiac, aortic, and gastrointestinal causes. Substernal pain radiating to the neck or arms is typical for myocardial ischemia.
keyPoints: Substernal pain with radiation to neck/jaw/shoulders suggests myocardial ischemia.
Radiation to both arms has a high association with MI.
Localized pain (demarcated by finger) is unusual for angina.
Retrosternal location may indicate esophageal pain.
Pain radiating to the back suggests acute aortic syndrome.

items.title: Pattern and Provoking/Alleviating Factors of Chest Discomfort
items.content: The pattern of chest discomfort, including onset, duration, and response to activity or position changes, helps differentiate between cardiac, aortic, and musculoskeletal causes. Myocardial ischemic pain typically builds over minutes and is relieved by rest.
keyPoints: Myocardial ischemic pain builds over minutes and is exacerbated by activity.
Immediate peak intensity suggests aortic dissection or pulmonary embolism.
Constant pain for hours/days without ECG/cardiac biomarker changes is unlikely myocardial ischemia.
Warm-up angina may occur with continued exertion.
Pericarditis pain worsens in supine position, relieved by sitting upright.

items.title: Postprandial Chest Discomfort and Its Implications
items.content: Chest discomfort that worsens after eating may indicate a gastrointestinal cause such as peptic ulcer disease, cholecystitis, or pancreatitis. Peptic ulcer disease typically presents symptoms 60–90 minutes post-meal. However, in patients with severe coronary atherosclerosis, blood flow redistribution to the splanchnic vasculature can trigger postprandial angina.
keyPoints: Peptic ulcer disease becomes symptomatic 60–90 min after meals.
Postprandial angina may occur due to blood flow redistribution in severe coronary atherosclerosis.
Physical exertion is unlikely to alter symptoms from gastrointestinal causes of chest pain.

items.title: Nitroglycerin Response and Differential Diagnosis
items.content: Relief of chest discomfort within minutes after nitroglycerin administration suggests but is not definitive for myocardial ischemia. Esophageal spasm may also be relieved by nitroglycerin. A delay of more than 10 minutes before relief indicates the symptoms are likely not due to ischemia or represent severe ischemia, such as during an acute MI.
keyPoints: Relief within minutes after nitroglycerin suggests but is not definitive for myocardial ischemia.
Esophageal spasm can also be relieved by nitroglycerin.
A delay of >10 min before relief suggests symptoms are not due to ischemia or represent severe ischemia.

items.title: Recognizing Hemodynamic Instability in Chest Discomfort
items.content: Significant tachycardia and hypotension are critical indicators of severe underlying conditions causing chest discomfort. These signs necessitate a rapid assessment for life-threatening issues such as acute myocardial infarction with cardiogenic shock, massive pulmonary embolism, pericarditis with tamponade, or tension pneumothorax.
keyPoints: Significant tachycardia and hypotension indicate severe hemodynamic consequences.
Prompt rapid survey for conditions like acute MI with cardiogenic shock, massive pulmonary embolism, pericarditis with tamponade, or tension pneumothorax.

items.title: Identifying Pulmonary Causes of Chest Discomfort
items.content: Pulmonary examination can localize primary pulmonary causes of chest discomfort. Conditions such as pneumonia, asthma, or pneumothorax may present with specific lung findings. Additionally, left ventricular dysfunction from severe ischemia/infarction and acute valvular complications can lead to pulmonary edema, indicating high risk.
keyPoints: Pulmonary examination localizes primary causes like pneumonia, asthma, or pneumothorax.
Left ventricular dysfunction and acute valvular complications may cause pulmonary edema.

items.title: Assessing Cardiac Findings in Chest Discomfort
items.content: Cardiac examination can reveal important findings such as jugular venous pulse patterns, heart sounds, and murmurs. These may indicate conditions like pericardial tamponade, myocardial dysfunction, or mechanical complications of STEMI. Murmurs may also suggest underlying cardiac disorders contributing to ischemia.
keyPoints: Jugular venous pulse may show patterns with pericardial tamponade or right ventricular dysfunction.
Heart sounds like third or fourth heart sound indicate myocardial dysfunction.
Murmurs can reveal mechanical complications of STEMI or underlying cardiac disorders.

items.title: Importance of Early ECG in Acute Chest Discomfort
items.content: An electrocardiogram (ECG) should be obtained within 10 minutes of presentation for patients with nontraumatic chest discomfort to identify those with ST-segment elevation indicative of myocardial infarction (MI), who require immediate intervention. Serial ECGs are recommended every 30–60 minutes in the early evaluation of suspected acute coronary syndrome (ACS).
keyPoints: ECG should be obtained within 10 min of presentation.
ST-segment elevation is diagnostic of MI, requiring immediate interventions.
Serial ECGs recommended every 30-60 min for suspected ACS.
Sensitivity for ischemia with resting ECG can be as low as 20%.

items.title: ECG and Chest Radiography in Differential Diagnosis of Chest Discomfort
items.content: ECG abnormalities such as ST-segment depression ≥0.2 mV or symmetric T-wave inversions indicate myocardial ischemia and higher risk for death or recurrent ischemia, while specific patterns can help differentiate conditions like pericarditis from acute MI. Chest radiography is useful in identifying pulmonary processes like pneumonia or pneumothorax but often shows unremarkable findings in ACS unless pulmonary edema is present.
keyPoints: ST-segment depression ≥0.2 mV and symmetric T-wave inversions indicate myocardial ischemia.
Diffuse ST-segment elevation with PR-segment depression suggests pericarditis over MI.
Chest radiography identifies pulmonary processes; unremarkable in ACS unless pulmonary edema is present.

items.title: Why High-Sensitivity Troponin Assays are Preferred for ACS Diagnosis
items.content: High-sensitivity troponin assays are preferred over previous generation assays due to their enhanced ability to detect myocardial injury, improving the accuracy of MI diagnosis and risk stratification in suspected ACS. These assays allow for rapid rule-out protocols with serial testing within 1–2 hours, providing a high negative predictive value.
keyPoints: Cardiac troponin is the preferred biomarker for diagnosing MI in patients with suspected ACS.
High-sensitivity troponin assays have greater analytical sensitivity than previous generation assays.
A very low concentration of cardiac troponin using a high-sensitivity assay may exclude MI with a negative predictive value >99% when presenting more than 2–3 hours after symptom onset.
Serial testing and changes in troponin concentration over 1-2 hours perform well for diagnosing ACS with high-sensitivity assays.

items.title: How to Interpret Troponin Levels in Acute Chest Pain Evaluation
items.content: In evaluating acute chest pain, it is crucial to measure troponin at presentation and repeat testing 1–3 hours later with high-sensitivity assays. Observation of changes between serial samples helps discriminate acute myocardial injury from chronic elevations due to other conditions. MI diagnosis requires a rising or falling pattern in troponin levels exceeding the 99th percentile reference limit, indicative of ischemic injury.
keyPoints: Troponin should be measured at presentation and repeated at 1–3 hours using high-sensitivity assays.
Changes between serial samples are necessary to differentiate acute myocardial injury from chronic elevations.
MI is diagnosed with a rising or falling troponin pattern exceeding the 99th percentile reference limit, indicating ischemic injury.

items.subtopic: INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
items.title: Use of Clinical Decision Pathways (CDPs) for Risk Stratification in ACS
items.content: Clinical decision pathways are recommended to categorize patients with acute nontraumatic chest pain into low, intermediate, and high risk. These pathways help identify patients with a low probability of ACS who can be safely discharged without further testing.
keyPoints: CDPs estimate the probability of ACS or major cardiac events during short-term follow-up.
Common elements across CDPs include symptoms typical for ACS, older age, risk factors for atherosclerosis, ischemic ECG abnormalities, and elevated cardiac troponin levels.
Integrated CDPs with ECGs and serial high-sensitivity cardiac troponin achieve miss rates for ACS of <0.5%.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING

items.subtopic: INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
items.title: Differentiating Diagnostic Algorithms from Prognostic Risk Scores in ACS
items.content: Clinicians should distinguish between decision-aids for diagnosing ACS and risk scores like TIMI and GRACE, which are used for prognosis after an ACS diagnosis.
keyPoints: Decision-aids estimate the probability of ACS or short-term major cardiac events.
Prognostic risk scores (e.g., TIMI, GRACE) are not intended for diagnostic assessment.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING

items.title: Role of Coronary CT Angiography in Intermediate-Risk Patients
items.content: Coronary CT angiography is recommended for patients with intermediate or undetermined risk after excluding other life-threatening causes. It has high sensitivity and a strong negative predictive value for obstructive coronary disease.
keyPoints: CT angiography is preferred for evaluating patients with low-intermediate probability of ACS.
It provides high negative predictive value by identifying no significant stenosis or plaque.
CT angiography can also exclude aortic dissection, pericardial effusion, and pulmonary embolism.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING

items.subtopic: Chest Discomfort | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY
items.question: What percentage of patients presenting to the ED with chest discomfort are eventually diagnosed with acute coronary syndrome (ACS)?
items.explanation: Fewer than 15% of evaluated patients are eventually diagnosed with ACS.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · Chest Discomfort | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: Chest Discomfort | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY
items.question: What is the estimated rate of major cardiovascular events through 30 days in low-risk patients with acute chest pain, excluding those with ST-segment elevation or definite noncardiac chest pain?
items.explanation: The estimated rate is 2.5% in a large population-based study.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · Chest Discomfort | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: Chest Discomfort | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY
items.question: What percentage of patients with chest discomfort of presumed nonischemic etiology who are discharged from the ED were later deemed to have had a missed myocardial infarction (MI)?
items.explanation: Historically, 2–6% of these patients were later deemed to have had a missed MI.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · Chest Discomfort | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND NATURAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.question: Which of the following best describes myocardial ischemia?
options: An imbalance between myocardial oxygen supply and demand, leading to chest discomfort.
A condition where there is an excess of myocardial oxygen supply over demand.
Myocardial injury due to physical exertion without any relation to oxygen supply.
A chronic condition characterized by persistent high levels of myocardial oxygen consumption.
items.explanation: Myocardial ischemia is caused by an imbalance between myocardial oxygen requirements and supply, resulting in chest discomfort.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.question: What characterizes stable angina?
options: Chest discomfort that occurs unpredictably and is relieved by rest.
Ischemic episodes precipitated by increased oxygen demand during exertion, relieved upon resting.
Severe chest pain lasting more than 30 minutes, not relieved by nitroglycerin.
Pain due to myocardial injury with ST-segment elevation on ECG.
items.explanation: Stable angina is characterized by ischemic episodes precipitated by increased oxygen demand during exertion and relieved upon resting.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.question: Which condition involves myocardial ischemia without detectable acute myocardial injury?
items.explanation: Unstable angina involves myocardial ischemia without detectable acute myocardial injury.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.question: What is the typical duration of chest discomfort associated with myocardial infarction (MI)?
options: Varies widely and cannot be specified
items.explanation: Chest discomfort associated with MI is commonly more severe, prolonged (usually lasting ≥30 min), and not relieved by rest.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.question: Which condition is often associated with chest discomfort described as 'tearing' in quality?
items.explanation: Acute aortic dissection is often described as causing severe, sudden onset chest discomfort that can be 'tearing' in quality.

items.question: What type of pain is typically associated with pericarditis?
options: Pleuritic, exacerbated by breathing and coughing
items.explanation: The pain of pericarditis is usually pleuritic and worsens with breathing, coughing, or changes in position.

items.question: What can cause chest discomfort associated with pulmonary embolism?
options: Distention of the pulmonary artery and right ventricular wall stress
items.explanation: Chest discomfort in pulmonary embolism can result from distention of the pulmonary artery or right ventricular wall stress.

items.question: Which condition is a rare cause of chest discomfort but may present with dyspnea and pleuritic symptoms?
items.explanation: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax is a rare cause of chest discomfort, often presenting with dyspnea and pleuritic symptoms.

items.subtopic: OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort
items.question: Which pulmonary condition is described as having chest pain similar to angina due to right ventricular myocardial ischemia?
items.explanation: Chronic pulmonary hypertension can manifest as chest pain similar to angina, suggesting right ventricular myocardial ischemia.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort

items.subtopic: OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort
items.question: What is the typical description of pleurisy-related chest pain?
options: A knifelike pain worsened by inspiration or coughing
items.explanation: Pleurisy is typically described as a knifelike pain that is worsened by inspiration or coughing.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort

items.subtopic: OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort
items.question: Which condition can cause chest tightness associated with breathlessness rather than pleurisy?
items.explanation: Reactive airways diseases can cause chest tightness associated with breathlessness rather than pleurisy.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort

items.question: Which quality of chest discomfort is most consistent with myocardial ischemic pain?
items.explanation: Pressure or tightness is typical of myocardial ischemic pain.

items.question: Which type of chest discomfort should prompt consideration of acute aortic syndrome?
options: Severe pain radiating to the back between the shoulder blades
Fleeting pain lasting only a few seconds
items.explanation: Severe pain radiating to the back, particularly between the shoulder blades, suggests acute aortic syndrome.

items.question: Which location of chest discomfort is typical for myocardial ischemic pain?
options: Highly localized demarcated by one finger
Substernal with radiation to the neck, jaw, shoulder, or arms
items.explanation: Myocardial ischemic pain typically presents as substernal with radiation.

items.question: Which pattern of chest discomfort is more suggestive of aortic dissection?
options: Builds over minutes and is exacerbated by activity
Fleeting, lasting only a few seconds
Constant for many hours to days
items.explanation: Pain that reaches its peak intensity immediately suggests aortic dissection.

items.question: Which symptom is most likely to be relieved promptly by acid-reducing therapies in the context of chest discomfort?
options: Acid reflux and peptic ulcer disease
items.explanation: The discomfort from acid reflux and peptic ulcer disease is usually diminished promptly by acid-reducing therapies.

items.question: What does a delay of more than 10 minutes before relief after nitroglycerin administration suggest?
options: Symptoms are not caused by ischemia or are due to severe ischemia
items.explanation: A delay of >10 min before relief after nitroglycerin suggests that the symptoms either are not caused by ischemia or are caused by severe ischemia, such as during acute MI.

items.question: Which condition should be considered if a patient presents with sudden onset of significant respiratory distress?
items.explanation: Sudden onset of significant respiratory distress should lead to consideration of pulmonary embolism and spontaneous pneumothorax.

items.question: Which physical examination finding is most indicative of a severe hemodynamic consequence in patients with chest discomfort?
options: Localized tenderness on the abdominal exam
items.explanation: Significant tachycardia and hypotension are indicative of important hemodynamic consequences.

items.question: What is a common auscultatory finding in patients with myocardial systolic or diastolic dysfunction?
items.explanation: A third or more commonly, a fourth heart sound reflects myocardial systolic or diastolic dysfunction.

items.question: Which condition may present with severe hypertension but can also be associated with profound hypotension?
items.explanation: Acute aortic emergencies usually present with severe hypertension but may be associated with profound hypotension.

items.question: What is the significance of unilateral lower-extremity swelling in patients with chest discomfort?
items.explanation: Unilateral lower-extremity swelling should raise suspicion about venous thromboembolism.

items.question: What is the primary goal of obtaining an ECG within 10 minutes of a patient presenting with nontraumatic chest discomfort?
options: To identify patients with ST-segment elevation diagnostic of MI for immediate interventions.
To diagnose pulmonary embolism based on sinus tachycardia and axis shift.
To detect myocardial ischemia using T-wave inversions.
To evaluate the presence of pericardial calcification.
items.explanation: The primary goal is to identify patients with ST-segment elevation diagnostic of MI for immediate interventions.

items.question: Which ECG findings are indicative of higher risk of death or recurrent ischemia in the absence of STEMI?
options: PR-segment depression and diffuse lead involvement.
Symmetric T-wave inversions at least 0.2 mV in depth and ST-segment depression.
S-wave in lead I with a Q-wave and T-wave in lead III.
items.explanation: ST-segment depression and symmetric T-wave inversions at least 0.2 mV are indicative of higher risk of death or recurrent ischemia.

items.question: What chest radiograph finding may be evident in patients with acute coronary syndrome (ACS)?
items.explanation: Pulmonary edema may be evident in patients with ACS, although findings are often unremarkable.

items.question: Which biomarker is preferred for diagnosing myocardial infarction (MI) in patients with suspected acute coronary syndrome (ACS)?
items.explanation: Cardiac troponin is the preferred biomarker for diagnosing MI in suspected ACS.

items.question: What advantage do high-sensitivity cardiac troponin assays offer over previous generation assays?
options: They have a greater negative predictive value for ruling out MI.
They detect myocardial injury only in ACS.
items.explanation: High-sensitivity assays have a greater negative predictive value, aiding in the evaluation of chest pain in the ED.

items.question: When using high-sensitivity troponin assays, what is sufficient to exclude MI with a negative predictive value >99%?
options: A very low concentration of cardiac troponin at presentation.
Multiple elevated troponin levels over 6 hours.
items.explanation: A very low concentration of cardiac troponin using a high-sensitivity assay at presentation can exclude MI with a negative predictive value >99%.

items.subtopic: INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
items.question: Which of the following elements are commonly used across multiple risk stratification tools for acute chest pain?
options: Symptoms typical for ACS, older age, and elevated cardiac troponin level
items.explanation: Common elements include symptoms typical for ACS, older age, risk factors for atherosclerosis, ischemic ECG abnormalities, and elevated cardiac troponin level.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING

items.subtopic: INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
items.question: What is the primary use of clinical decision pathways (CDPs) in managing patients with acute nontraumatic chest pain?
options: To provide immediate treatment for ACS
To categorize patients as low, intermediate, and high risk
items.explanation: CDPs are used to categorize patients based on their risk of having ACS.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING

items.subtopic: INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
items.question: Which modality is preferred for evaluating patients with intermediate or undetermined risk after excluding other life-threatening causes?
items.explanation: Coronary CT angiography is recommended for diagnostic coronary imaging in such patients.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING

items.subtopic: INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
items.question: What is the major strength of coronary CT angiography in evaluating chest pain?
options: High sensitivity for detecting non-obstructive coronary disease
Positive predictive value for significant stenosis
Negative predictive value for no significant stenosis or plaque
items.explanation: The major strength of CT angiography is its negative predictive value in finding no significant stenosis or coronary plaque.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING

items.explanation: More than 60% of patients with chest discomfort are hospitalized.

items.statement: The majority of patients presenting with acute chest pain in the ED are diagnosed with a life-threatening cardiopulmonary condition.
items.explanation: As few as 5% have other life-threatening cardiopulmonary conditions.

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.statement: Unstable ischemic heart disease is classified as unstable angina when there is evidence of acute myocardial injury.
items.explanation: Unstable ischemic heart disease is classified as unstable angina when there is no detectable acute myocardial injury.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.statement: The term 'acute coronary syndrome' includes conditions like unstable angina, NSTEMI, and STEMI.
items.explanation: 'Acute coronary syndrome' encompasses unstable angina, NSTEMI, and STEMI.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.statement: Pain from pericarditis is primarily due to direct irritation of the pericardium.
items.explanation: The pain of pericarditis arises principally from associated pleural inflammation, not direct irritation of the pericardium.

items.statement: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy can mimic acute myocardial infarction due to similar ECG changes.
items.explanation: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy often presents with chest pain and shortness of breath, along with ST-segment elevation on ECG, mimicking an acute MI.

items.subtopic: OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort
items.statement: Gastrointestinal disorders are the most common cause of nontraumatic chest discomfort.
items.explanation: The passage states that gastrointestinal disorders are the most common cause of nontraumatic chest discomfort.

items.subtopic: OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort
items.statement: Costochondritis is a rare condition causing chest pain.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that costochondritis, also known as Tietze's syndrome, is relatively common.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · OTHER CARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES (part 2) | NONCARDIOPULMONARY CAUSES | Chest Discomfort

items.statement: The severity of chest discomfort has high diagnostic accuracy for myocardial ischemia.
items.explanation: Severity of discomfort has poor diagnostic accuracy.

items.statement: Pain that is highly localized, demarcated by the tip of one finger, is typical for angina.
items.explanation: Highly localized pain is unusual for angina.

items.statement: Physical exertion is very unlikely to alter symptoms from gastrointestinal causes of chest pain.
items.explanation: In contrast with its impact in some patients with angina, physical exertion is very unlikely to alter symptoms from gastrointestinal causes of chest pain.

items.statement: Dyspnea is a discriminative symptom for myocardial ischemia.
items.explanation: Dyspnea may occur with multiple conditions considered in the differential diagnosis of chest pain and thus is not discriminative.

items.statement: An unremarkable physical exam definitively reassures the absence of unstable ischemic heart disease.
items.explanation: Findings on physical examination may be normal in patients with unstable ischemic heart disease.

items.statement: A murmur of aortic insufficiency can indicate ascending aortic dissection.
items.explanation: A murmur of aortic insufficiency may be a complication of ascending aortic dissection.

items.statement: The sensitivity of the resting ECG for detecting ischemia is high.
items.explanation: The sensitivity of the resting ECG for ischemia is poor, as low as 20% in some studies.

items.statement: Chest radiography can identify pericardial calcification in chronic pericarditis.
items.explanation: Pericardial calcification is a specific finding on chest radiographs in chronic pericarditis.

items.statement: It is necessary to measure troponin in all patients presenting with chest pain, regardless of ACS suspicion.
items.explanation: Troponin measurement is not necessary unless there's suspicion of ACS or for specific risk stratification.

items.statement: High-sensitivity troponin assays can detect myocardial injury in non-ACS cardiopulmonary conditions.
items.explanation: With high sensitivity, these assays detect myocardial injury more frequently even in non-ACS conditions.

items.subtopic: INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
items.statement: TIMI and GRACE risk scores are used for diagnostic assessment of ACS.
items.explanation: These risk scores are designed for stratification of prognosis, not for diagnostic assessment.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING

items.subtopic: INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
items.statement: Stress nuclear perfusion imaging and stress echocardiography offer superior diagnostic performance over exercise ECG.
items.explanation: These functional tests provide better diagnostic accuracy than exercise ECG.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.assertion: Myocardial ischemia can be caused by increased myocardial oxygen demand.
items.reason: Increased myocardial oxygen demand during intense psychological stress can precipitate unstable ischemic symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.subtopic: CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY
items.assertion: Myocardial ischemia always results in ST-segment elevation on ECG.
items.reason: ST-elevation MI (STEMI) is a type of myocardial injury that involves ST-segment elevation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because not all myocardial ischemia results in ST-segment elevation; the reason is true but does not explain the incorrect assertion.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · CAUSES OF CHEST DISCOMFORT | MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA/INJURY

items.assertion: Acute aortic dissection often presents with chest pain that radiates to the back.
items.reason: Dissections involving the ascending aorta can extend to the descending aorta, causing pain from the front of the chest to between the shoulder blades.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why acute aortic dissection often presents with radiating back pain.

items.assertion: Pulmonary embolism can cause chest discomfort due to involvement of the pericardium.
items.reason: The chest discomfort in pulmonary embolism results from pleural surface involvement and distention of the pulmonary artery.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; pulmonary embolism does not cause chest discomfort due to pericardial involvement.

items.assertion: Pain that builds over minutes and is exacerbated by activity suggests myocardial ischemia.
items.reason: Myocardial ischemic discomfort usually builds over minutes and is exacerbated by activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Pain that reaches its peak intensity immediately is typical of myocardial ischemia.
items.reason: Pain reaching peak intensity immediately suggests aortic dissection or pulmonary embolism.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; such pain is not typical for myocardial ischemia.

items.assertion: Pulmonary edema is an indicator of high risk in patients with chest discomfort.
items.reason: Left ventricular dysfunction from severe ischemia/infarction can lead to pulmonary edema.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Chest wall tenderness excludes myocardial ischemia.
items.reason: Palpation of the chest wall often elicits pain in patients with various musculoskeletal conditions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because chest wall tenderness does not exclude myocardial ischemia.

items.subtopic: INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
items.assertion: CT angiography is preferred for patients with known obstructive epicardial disease.
items.reason: Stress nuclear perfusion imaging and stress echocardiography are preferred in such cases.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING

items.subtopic: INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
items.assertion: Clinical decision pathways can be used to discharge patients with low clinical probability of ACS.
items.reason: CDPs are designed to identify high-risk patients for immediate intervention.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as CDPs help identify low-risk patients.
items.reference: Chest Discomfort · INTEGRATIVE DECISION-AIDS | CORONARY AND MYOCARDIAL STRESS IMAGING
